content:
AUDIENCE:
This reader is a senior business executive, likely in their 50s or 60s, with extensive experience in leadership and decision-making but no formal background in science or medicine. They are practical, results-focused, and pressed for time, preferring clear, concise explanations free of jargon. They understand basic health concepts but may be unfamiliar with detailed biological processes. They care about how vaccines impact public health, business continuity, and economic stability. Overly technical or lengthy explanations would lose their interest, as would anything that feels speculative or uncertain. A confident, straightforward tone that respects their intelligence while making the science accessible will resonate best.

===BEGIN===
Vaccines work by training your immune system to recognize and fight specific viruses or bacteria without causing illness. Think of it as a rehearsal: the vaccine introduces a harmless piece of the germ, prompting your body to prepare defenses—like creating protective “memory” cells. If you later encounter the real germ, your immune system quickly recognizes and attacks it, preventing serious illness. This process helps protect not only individuals but also the workforce and the broader economy by reducing disease spread and keeping businesses running smoothly. In short, vaccines equip your body’s natural defenses in advance, so you’re ready when real threats come along.
===END===
